titleStmt.title: Letter from Peter Mooney to his sister Katie Mooney, 2 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Peter Mooney

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Peter Mooney was born 5 February 1886 in Folistown, Dunboyne, Co Meath. Orphaned at age 7, he and his sister Katie (b. 1884) were raised by their uncle William and his wife Mary in Caulstown. While there are no surviving enlistment papers for Peter, his War Badge Index Record shows his enlistment date as 18 October 1915.
He served with the Royal Artillery and later transferred to the Royal Engineers where he was promoted to the rank of Sergeant. He served in several places at the Western Front, including Ypres, St. Omer and Passchendael. Peter and Kate were avid correspondents. This letter is from a collection of over ninety surviving letters.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Nov 2nd 1915 Dear Katie I was glad to
receive your letter this
morning. I also got one
from John Farrell & James
Reilly. It does me good
to know that they think
of me. As I lie in bed
at night I think of those
at home. My arm went
on all right and I will
not have to be done
again.
2)
When you are writing
again send me on some
corn plaster as I will
want some now. Tell
the Priests at the Station
that I was asking for
them. I am getting more
used to life here every
day. It will come very
easy when we get over
the drill. John Farrell
says he does not like
going over to Caulstown
as he thinks he should
see me. Keep Poor old
Barry well fed I know
he can eat a lot
3)
We will have a good time
all to-gether when I get
home at Christmas. Tell
all the lads that I expect
to have a good time with
them on St. Stephens Night.
I hope they keep hurling
every Sunday in the field.
I am glad that Charly
is minding the Horses
& Greyhounds so well. Tell him I will not forget
to him. Tell him to keep
the place nice & clean that
I said it. I did not
write to Tommy yet
but will soon. I have an
awful lot of writing to do.
I will write to all in turns.
Has Paddy learned to ride
the Bycycle yet I hope
he has. Tell Mary & Joe
Fitzpatrick that I was
asking for them. I am
sure you are getting on well
without me now. Do not
fret for me. You make
me happy when I know
that you are all happy & well. Good Bye & God Bless you all Your Fond Brother Peter Mooney
